notes.title: Central bank credibility
notes.text: A central bank free to re-optimize each period is tempted to surprise-inflate. Rational agents foresee this, so the economy lands at high inflation with no extra output. The remedy is to precommit to a binding rule rather than retain discretion.

notes.title: Rule vs act utilitarianism
notes.text: An agent free to re-evaluate each act can be pushed toward monstrous one-offs, like punishing an innocent to placate a mob. Committing to a standing rule protects the trust that case-by-case optimization would erode.

notes.title: Bacterial quorum sensing
notes.text: Cells secrete signaling molecules; once their concentration crosses a density threshold, the whole colony switches behavior simultaneously, coordinating as if it were one organism.

notes.title: Bank runs
notes.text: Each depositor's incentive to withdraw depends on how many others are withdrawing. Past a threshold of withdrawals, confidence collapses and the whole system flips into a run all at once.

notes.title: Induced traffic demand
notes.text: Adding road lanes lowers the perceived cost of a trip, so latent demand floods back until congestion returns to its prior level. Expanding supply does not relieve the binding constraint.

notes.title: Natural rate of unemployment
notes.text: Pushing unemployment below its natural rate lowers the perceived cost of inflation, so wage- and price-setters revise expectations upward until unemployment snaps back to the natural rate.

notes.title: Escalation of commitment
notes.text: Having sunk resources into a failing project, managers reframe sunk costs as recoverable and pour in more to avoid conceding the original decision was wrong.

notes.title: Veil of ignorance
notes.text: Strip a chooser of knowledge of their own position in society and, under uncertainty, they design institutions that protect the worst-off floor.
